title: Condemned to Freedom
body:
When Nietzsche proclaimed the death of god in 1882, he was proclaiming captivity to the free. With the regrettable triumph of rationalism over deism in the 19th century, mankind was doomed to be ruled by itself - "condemned to freedom" (Evola). Humans are in a perpetual mimetic competition as developed by Girard. The REAL God has provided for us a real and effectual scapegoat to satisfy the mimetic triangle eternally, however, if God doesn't exist we must be the ones to provide the essential scapegoat. Therefore, if there is no God to govern us, regulate our tendencies, and dictate our actions, we must become god. And as gods, we must find a sacrifice to perpetuate peace on earth.

So, as humanity has taken the place of god in the postmodern consciousness, they have also condemned themselves to a perpetual striving for whatever object the mimelic current determines is the next prize and thus a never-ending competition amongst themselves resulting in an ultimate sacrifice of anything transcendent and lofty.

title: Politics Care About You
body:
Strauss and Aristotle agree that to be human is to philosophical, and to be philosophical is to be political. There is a notion in our world today that says an individual can "mind their own business" without caring about "politics" (whatever they mean by that) and avoid engaging in them. At the same time, they make alliances with coworkers, create pacts family and friends, and use leverage at work to get more days off. We are political animals (Aristotle). There might have been a time some 50 years ago when a corporate job could pay a mortgage, support a large family, and the family man could just keep his head down and go to-and-from the office without making so much as a peep outside of his concentric circles of influence. However, those days are gone. To be anything other than a peon in the 2020's means distinguishing yourself, risking comfort for failure, and speaking your mind. 

Moreover, for those of us that think there is more to life than making money for the purpose of pleasure, politics is the ubiquitous route to meaning. A young man finds meaning in several different arenas of life - family, friends, church, education, his job - but politics differs from all these in one significant way: "Politics is war without bloodshed, while war is politics with bloodshed" (Zedong). As creatures designed in the image of God and birthed from the Faustian cullure of Western civilization, we have an innate desire for striving and competifion (especially as men). War is no doubt a regrettable necessily, however, politics is the way in which nations achieve dominance and superiority over their enemies without the need to spill his blood. For these reasons and a thousand more, politics should be embraced and practiced by any who would like to wrest control over their future rather than having another dictate it.
